subject: UX & Research
title: The Serial Position Effect
description: A micro-lesson that makes The Serial Position Effect usable.

hook.text: Give someone a list of 20 words. They'll remember the first few and the last few. The middle vanishes.

buildup.text: The primacy effect (first items) and recency effect (last items) create a U-shaped recall curve.

discovery.text: Ebbinghaus discovered this in 1885. It explains why users remember the first and last menu items best.

twist.text: Navigation bars exploit this: Home goes first, key CTAs go last. The middle items get far less clicks.

climax.text: Place your most important actions at the start or end of any list, never buried in the middle.

punchline.text: First and last get remembered. The middle gets lost.

quiz.question: What does the serial position effect predict?
options: People best remember items at the start and end of a list
Lists are always better than grids
Longer lists are easier to remember
